elements.sent-2411-001: Let $T$ be a rooted tree in which a set $M$ of vertices are marked.
elements.sent-2411-002: The lowest common ancestor (LCA) of $M$ is the unique vertex $\ell$ with the following property: after failing (i.e., deleting) any single vertex $x$ from $T$, the root remains connected to $\ell$ if and only if it remains connected to some marked vertex.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this note, we introduce a generalized notion called $f$-fault-equivalent LCAs ($f$-FLCA), obtained by adapting the above view to $f$ failures for arbitrary $f \geq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show that there is a unique vertex set $M^* = \FLCA(M,f)$ of minimal size such after the failure of any $f$ vertices (or less), the root remains connected to some $v \in M$ iff it remains connected to some $u \in M^*$.
elements.sent-2411-006: A bound of $|M^*| \leq 2^{f-1}$ always holds, regardless of $|M|$, and holds with equality for some choice of $T$ and $M$.
elements.sent-2411-008: There is an $n$-vertex tree $T$, rooted at a source $s$.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this tree, a nonempty and possibly large vertex subset of interest $M \subseteq V(T)$ is marked.
elements.sent-2411-010: We are preparing for the future failures (or faults) of at most $f$ currently unknown vertices, which will be deleted from the tree.
elements.sent-2411-011: Namely, we will want to answer the following question: Is there some marked vertex $v \in M$ that remains reachable from $s$ in $T - F$? However, we would like to save on memory costs, and avoid storing the entire set $M$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Instead, we want to preprocesses $M$ to find and store a smaller ``representative'' set of vertices $M^* \subseteq V(T)$, which is equivalent to $M$ in terms of the above question.
elements.sent-2411-013: Namely, for every fault-set $F \subseteq V(F)$ with $|F| \leq f$, all vertices in $M^*$ are disconnected from $s$ in $T-F$ if and only if this is true for the original marked set $M$.
elements.sent-2411-014: We now introduce some definitions to formalize the above.
elements.sent-2411-015: First, we define the covering relation.
elements.sent-2411-016: For two vertex sets $A, B \subseteq V(T)$, we say that $A$ covers $B$, and denote $A \covers B$, if for every $b \in B$ there exists $a \in A$ which is an ancestor of $b$ in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Note that the failure of $F \subseteq V(T)$ disconnects $s$ from all of $M$ iff $F \covers M$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Our requirement from the representative set $M^*$ is that this should happen iff $F \covers M^*$, whenever $|F| \leq f$.
elements.sent-2411-019: We therefore define the $f$-fault equivalence relation.
elements.sent-2411-020: For $f \geq 1$, we say that two vertex sets $M,N \subseteq V(T)$ are $f$-fault-equivalent, and denote $M \sim_f N$, if for any $F \subseteq V(T)$ with $|F| \leq f$, it holds that $F \covers M$ iff $F \covers N$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Our motivating problem can now be succinctly stated as follows: \begin{problem Given the tree $T$, the marked set $\emptyset = M \subseteq V(T)$ and a fault parameter $f \geq 1$, find a set $M^* \subseteq V(T)$ of minimal size such that $M^* \sim_f M $.. \end{problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: When preparing for a single vertex failure, i.e., when $f = 1$, a moment's reflection will show that one can always choose $M^*$ having only one vertex: the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of all marked vertices, denoted $\LCA(M)$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Indeed, a single failed vertex $v$ disconnects all of $M$ from $s$ iff $\{v\} \covers M$, namely iff $v$ is a common ancestor of all marked vertices, which happens iff $v$ is an ancestor of $\LCA(M)$, i.e., iff $\{v\} \covers \{\LCA(M)\}$.
elements.sent-2411-024: In fact, $\LCA(M)$ is the only single vertex satisfying this property.
elements.sent-2411-025: This means that $\LCA(M)$ could be equivalently defined by the unique optimal solution to \Cref{problem with $f=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Thus, letting $f$ increase beyond $1$ yields a generalized notion of LCA given by the optimal solution to \Cref{problem .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Also, as we will show, the optimal solution $M^*$ is always unique, and consists of LCAs of subsets of $M$.
elements.sent-2411-028: For these reasons, we call $M^*$ the $f$-fault LCA of $M$, and denote it by $\FLCA(M, f)$.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this note, we give a simple algorithm to compute $\FLCA(M,f)$, the optimal solution for \Cref{problem , and answer a natural question of interest (given our "memory savings" motivation): how small can $\FLCA(M,f)$ be? When $f = 1$, we saw it has size $1$, regardless of how large $M$ is.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This extends to a bound of $2^{f-1}$ on the size of $\FLCA(M,f)$, which is worst-case optimal (i.e., for some choice of $T,M$, $|\FLCA(M,f)| = 2^{f-1}$).
elements.sent-2411-031: Thus, when the fault parameter $f$ is constant, we can represent any marked vertex set $M$ (in the sense of $f$-fault equivalence) using only a constant number of representative vertices.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our results are summarized in the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-033: Let $T$ be an $n$-vertex tree rooted at vertex $s$, $M \subseteq V(T)$ be a non-empty set of marked vertices, and $f \geq 1$.. The following hold:.
elements.sent-2411-034: There is a unique set $\FLCA(M,f)$ having minimal size among all the $f$-fault-equivalent sets to $M$, namely among $\{N \subseteq V(T) \mid N \sim_f M\}$.
elements.sent-2411-035: It holds that $|\FLCA(M,f)| \leq 2^{f-1}$, and this bound is tight.
elements.sent-2411-036: That is, for some choice of $T$ and $M$, this holds with equality.
elements.sent-2411-037: There is an $O(n)$ time algorithm to compute $\FLCA(M,f)$ given $T$, $M$ and $f$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Further, after $O(n)$ time for preprocessing $T$, one can compute $\FLCA(M,f)$ within $O(|M|)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-039: We remark that considering failures of edges instead of vertices, or even allowing a mixture of failing vertices and edges, does not change our results regarding $\FLCA(M,f)$.
elements.sent-2411-040: To state this explicitly: $\FLCA(M,f)$ is the unique vertex set $M^* \subseteq V(T)$ having minimal size such that for every $F \subseteq V(T) \cup E(T)$ of size $|F| \leq f$, in $T-F$ it holds that the root $s$ is connected to some $v \in M$ iff it is connected to some $u \in M^*$.
elements.sent-2411-041: This is due to the fact that, in terms of connectivity to the root, the failure of an edge in a tree has the same effect as the failure of its lower endpoint.
elements.sent-2411-042: It is easy to prove that the function $\varphi (\cdot) = \FLCA(\cdot, f)$ admits the following nice aggregation property: $\varphi(A \cup B) = \varphi (A \cup \varphi(B))$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Such aggregation properties are often exploited for efficient computations.
elements.sent-2411-044: As a ``toy example'', suppose the marked set $M$ is revealed to us over time in batches $M_1, M_2, M_3, \dots$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Then we can save on memory in the time between batches $t$ and $t+1$, only (at most $2^{f-1}$) vertices in $M^*_t = \FLCA(M_1 \cup \cdots \cup M_t, f)$.
elements.sent-2411-046: When $M_{t+1}$ arrives, we can use the aggregation property and compute $M^*_{t+1}$ as $\FLCA(M^*_t \cup M_{t+1 , f)$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The notions and results presented in this note were developed during research on fault-tolerant graph data structures, but eventually did not make their way into the final solutions.
elements.sent-2411-048: Still, the author believes they could be of potential use in the field of fault-tolerant graph structures and algorithms, and hopes such applications would be found in the future.
elements.sent-2411-049: The proof is by analyzing the following algorithm for computing $\FLCA(M,f)$.
elements.sent-2411-050: The notation $T_v$ stands for the subtree of $T$ rooted at vertex $v$.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ for computing $\FLCA(M,f)$}\label{alg:FLCA} Input: Rooted tree $T$, non-empty vertex set $M \subseteq V(T)$, integer $f \geq 1$ Output: Vertex set $\mathcal{A}(T,M,f) \subseteq V(T)$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\ell \gets \LCA(M)$ \If{$\ell \in M$} \label{line:l-in-M} \Return $\{\ell\}$ \EndIf \State $u_1 , \dots, u_d \gets$ the children of $\ell$ with $M \cap V(T_{u_i}) \neq \emptyset$ \Comment{Note:\ $d \geq 2$ as $\LCA(M) = \ell \notin M$} \label{line:children-of-l} \If{$d > f$} \label{line:d-vs-f} \Return $\{\ell\}$ \EndIf \State $M_1, \dots, M_d \gets M \cap V(T_{u_1}), \dots, M \cap V(T_{u_k})$ \State \Return $\bigcup_{i=1}^d \mathcal{A}(T, M_i, f-d+1)$ \Comment{Note:\ $1 \leq f-d+1 \leq f-1$} \label{line:recurse} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-051: We divide the proof into several claims regarding algorithm $\mathcal{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-052: All of them are proved by strong induction on $f$.
elements.sent-2411-053: We denote the output as $M^* = \mathcal{A (T,M,f)$, and in case \Cref{line:recurse is executed, we also denote $M^*_i = \mathcal{A (T, M_i, f-d+1)$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-112: Algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ can be implemented in $O(n)$ time by dynamic programming on the tree $T$.
elements.sent-2411-113: As an improvement, we show after $O(n)$ time for preprocessing $T$, one can answer queries $(M,f)$ by computing $\FLCA(M,f)$ within $O(|M|)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-114: To this end, We build two classical data structures for (pairwise) LCA and level ancestor queries:.
elements.sent-2411-115: This requires $O(n)$ time, and queries can be answered in $O(1)$ time~\cite{BenderF00,BenderF04 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: Additionally, we create a lookup table $D$ for vertices, where $D[v]$ stores a ``switch bit'' initialized to zero, and a pointer initialized to a null value.
elements.sent-2411-117: This concludes the preprocessing, taking $O(n)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-118: Given a query $(M,f)$ with $M = \{v_1, \dots, v_{|M|}\}$, we now explain the implementation details for executing $\mathcal{A (T,M,f)$ in $O(|M|)$ time.}
elements.sent-2411-119: Computing $\ell$ takes $O(|M|)$ time by initializing $\ell \gets \lca(v_1, v_2)$ and updating $\ell \gets \lca(\ell, v_i)$ for $i= 3, \dots, |M|$.

elements.sent-2411-121: We aim to store $u_1, \dots, u_d$ in a linked list $L$, and to store each $M_i$ in a linked list pointed to from $D[u_i]$.
elements.sent-2411-122: We sequentially process each $v_i \in M$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-124: Next, we do a lookup to $D[u]$.
elements.sent-2411-125: If the switch bit is $0$, we (i) change it to $1$, (ii) change the pointer of $D[u]$ to the head of a new linked list of length $1$ that stores $v_i$, and (iii) add $u$ to the linked list $L$.
elements.sent-2411-126: If the switch bit is $1$, we just add $v$ to the linked list pointed by $D[u]$ (that was created when the switch was turned on).
elements.sent-2411-128: To invoke the recursive calls, We scan the linked list $L$ containing $u_1, \dots, u_d$ (where this is their order in $L$).
elements.sent-2411-129: When processing $u_i$, we ``clean up'' the lookup table entry $D[u_i]$ by copying the pointer to the linked list containing $M_i$, then reverting $D[u_i]$ to its original state (switch bit $0$ and null pointer).
elements.sent-2411-130: We can now execute the recursive calls $\mathcal{A (T,M_i,f-d+1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-131: This ensures that the lookup table $D$ returns to its cleaned state after the current query $(M,f)$ is answered, allowing us to treat any future query $(M',f')$ in the same manner.
